##### II.2.a.a — AI expects catastrophe: the knowing optimizer

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** The canonical, crowded leaf. The Sharp Left Turn (Yudkowsky/Soares, prior-dominant, first-critical-try) and Architectural Agency (Bengio, Russell) share it and split on the agency crux, a prescription difference. Links trimmed to four.
:::

This is the leaf most people picture, and the most crowded: a capable system that understands its action is catastrophic and takes it anyway. It is the canonical failure. Two worldviews share it, agree almost completely, and split only on what to do.

The Sharp Left Turn is the first. In December 2024 Anthropic and Redwood documented ["alignment faking"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.14093), and Apollo found models scheming in-context when it served a goal — the empirical face of an old argument, instrumental convergence: any capable optimizer develops the same subgoals, so power-seeking is emergent, the timeline compresses, and the system moves before anyone sees the gap, which makes [iterative safety testing impossible](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/uMQ3cqWDPHhjtiesc/agi-ruin-a-list-of-lethalities). Yudkowsky and Soares hold it, the phrase is Soares's, they reason from priors rather than current behavior, and they trust only a hard stop.

Bengio shares almost all of that and lands on the same leaf, but answers one crux differently: is dangerous agency emergent, or a separable choice? A model querying a database is an oracle; the same model in a loop with tools and an open goal is an agent, and the danger is the second. So Bengio would build the oracle, a [non-agentic "Scientist AI" that models without acting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.15657), where Yudkowsky would not build at all. Stuart Russell belongs here for the same reason, his [CIRL](https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.03137) building deference into the objective. Same failure, opposite prescription, and the whole distance between them is that one crux.
